title: Full AI Stack — Agent Potato: License to Chat

steps.title: Welcome to the Lab
steps.instruction:
Welcome! In this hands-on lab you'll use agent mode to build a complete feature across a full-stack codebase: React on the front end, Express and SQLite on the back end.

We've cloned the OctoCAT Supply project for you. Follow the steps in this panel as you advance through the slides.

steps.title: Explore the Project Structure
steps.instruction:
Open the Explorer panel and take a look at the project. You should see:

• frontend/ — React 18 + TypeScript + Tailwind
• api/ — Express.js + TypeScript + SQLite
• Makefile — Unified build commands
• docker-compose.yml — Multi-service setup

Don't dive into the files yet. We're going to let agent mode do the reconnaissance for us.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Explorer: Ctrl+Shift+E

steps.title: Agent Mode Reconnaissance
steps.instruction:
Open Copilot Chat and make sure the Agent mode is selected from the mode dropdown at the top of the chat panel.

Type:

What does this project do? Walk me through the folder structure, tech stack, and how to run it locally.

Watch as agent mode searches your codebase, reads key files, and assembles a comprehensive overview.

steps.title: Drill Into the API
steps.instruction:
Now ask a more targeted question:

How is the API organized? What routes exist and what do they do? Show me the database schema.

Agent mode builds on the context from your previous question. Notice how each question deepens your understanding without you opening a single file.

steps.title: Check the Frontend
steps.instruction:
Ask one more exploration question:

What React pages and components exist in the frontend? How is routing configured?

By now you should have a solid mental model of the entire codebase. You explored a full-stack project without touching the Explorer. This is reconnaissance with agent mode.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Review Approval Gates
steps.instruction:
Let's see the approval system in action. In agent mode, type:

Run make test to check the current test status of the project

Agent mode will ask for your approval before running the terminal command. Review the command and click Continue to approve it.

This is the safety net: every terminal command requires your explicit consent.
steps.tip: You can configure auto-approve in settings, but start with manual approval to understand what commands the agent runs.

steps.title: Check Auto-Approve Settings
steps.instruction:
Open Settings and search for github.copilot.chat.agent.autoApprove. This controls whether agent mode runs terminal commands without asking.

Leave it off for now — manual approval teaches you what commands the agent wants to run.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar and settings tab for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• Settings: Ctrl+,

steps.title: Discover Mode Commands
steps.instruction:
Open the Command Palette and type Chat: Change Mode to see the available commands for switching between Ask, Edit, and Agent modes.

Close the palette — we'll switch modes using the dropdown next.

steps.title: Try Ask Mode
steps.instruction:
Switch to Ask mode using the mode dropdown. Type:

What testing framework does this project use? Where do the tests live?

Notice that Ask mode gives you an answer but does not create or modify any files. This is the safe exploration mode.

steps.title: Try Edit Mode
steps.instruction:
Switch to Edit mode. Open api/routes/products.ts in the editor, then type:

Add JSDoc comments to all exported functions in this file

Edit mode proposes diffs that you accept or reject individually. It's precise and targeted.

steps.title: Back to Agent Mode
steps.instruction:
Switch back to Agent mode for the rest of the lab. You now know when to reach for each mode:

• Ask — exploration and questions
• Edit — targeted single-file changes
• Agent — multi-step, multi-file builds

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Command Palette: Ctrl+Shift+P
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Create the Instructions File
steps.instruction:
We're going to create a .github/copilot-instructions.md file that encodes this project's conventions. Open a new file and save it as .github/copilot-instructions.md.

Type the following:

# Copilot Instructions — OctoCAT Supply

## Language & Framework
- TypeScript with strict mode for both frontend and backend
- React 18 with functional components and hooks
- Express.js for the API layer

## Testing
- Use Vitest for all tests
- Run tests with make test

steps.title: Add Database Rules
steps.instruction:
Below your existing instructions, add database-specific rules:

## Database
- SQLite via better-sqlite3
- Always use parameterized queries
- Wrap multi-table operations in transactions

## Style
- Use Tailwind CSS utility classes for frontend
- Prefer named exports over default exports
- Use async/await, never callbacks

Save the file. These instructions will shape every Copilot response from now on.

steps.title: Verify Instructions Are Active
steps.instruction:
Open Copilot Chat in agent mode and ask:

What instructions have you loaded for this workspace?

Copilot should reference your new copilot-instructions.md file. From here on, every response will follow your project's conventions.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Create a Scoped Instruction File
steps.instruction:
Create another file at .github/instructions/react-components.instructions.md:

---
applyTo: "frontend/src/**/*.tsx"
---

# React Component Instructions

- Use functional components with hooks
- Fetch data with React Query (useQuery)
- Use Tailwind utility classes for styling
- Include TypeScript types for all props

These rules apply only when working on frontend TSX files.
steps.tip: Scoped instructions are additive. They combine with your repo-wide instructions, they don't replace them.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the editor tab for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N

steps.title: Build the Backend Endpoint
steps.instruction:
Time to build. In agent mode, type:

Create a new GET /api/reports/inventory endpoint that returns current stock levels grouped by category. Follow the route pattern in api/routes/products.ts. Register the route in the API entry point. Create a Vitest test file. Run make test to verify everything passes.

Watch agent mode plan the task, create files, register the route, write tests, and run them.

steps.title: Review the Output
steps.instruction:
Agent mode should have created at least two files: the route file and its test. Look at the diffs in the chat panel. Verify that:

• The route follows the same pattern as products.ts
• Database queries use parameterized statements
• The test file uses Vitest, not Jest
• The route is registered in the API entry point

If something looks off, tell the agent. It will fix it in the next iteration.

steps.title: Fix Any Test Failures
steps.instruction:
If any tests failed, simply tell agent mode:

The inventory tests are failing. Read the error output and fix the issues.

Agent mode will read the test output, diagnose the problem, edit the code, and re-run the tests. This iterate-until-green loop is one of agent mode's strongest patterns.
steps.tip: If the agent is stuck in a loop, provide more specific direction: point it at the exact error line or suggest a specific fix.

steps.title: Verify Tests in Terminal
steps.instruction:
Open the Terminal and run make test yourself to confirm the test suite passes independently of agent mode.

Verifying outside the agent's loop builds confidence that the code is genuinely working, not just passing within the agent's session.

steps.title: Review Changes in Source Control
steps.instruction:
Open the Source Control panel to see every file agent mode created or modified. Review the diffs — this is the same view you would use before committing.

Look for files you didn't expect. Agent mode sometimes creates helper files or modifies configuration files beyond what you asked for.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• Terminal: Ctrl+`
• Source Control: Ctrl+Shift+G

steps.title: Build the Frontend Component
steps.instruction:
Stay in agent mode and type:

Create a React component at frontend/src/pages/InventoryReport.tsx that fetches data from GET /api/reports/inventory and displays it in a table. Use the same data fetching pattern as the existing pages. Add a route for /reports/inventory in the router config and a navigation link in the sidebar.

Agent mode will read an existing page component, create the new file, update the router, and add navigation.

steps.title: Verify the Frontend
steps.instruction:
Ask agent mode to start the development servers:

Run make dev to start both the API and frontend servers

After approving the terminal command, check that:
• The API serves data at /api/reports/inventory
• The frontend page renders and displays the data
• The navigation link appears in the sidebar
steps.tip: If the component doesn't render correctly, describe the problem to agent mode. "The table is empty even though the API returns data" gives it enough to diagnose the issue.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Write Comprehensive Tests
steps.instruction:
Ask agent mode to improve the test coverage:

Write additional unit tests for the inventory endpoint. Test the happy path with valid data, the empty result case, and the case where the database query fails. Follow the testing patterns in the existing test files.

Let agent mode write the tests, run them, and iterate on failures.

steps.title: Run the Full Test Suite
steps.instruction:
Ask agent mode to run the complete test suite:

Run make test to execute all tests in the project. If any fail, fix them.

This verifies that your new code doesn't break existing functionality. Agent mode will fix any regressions automatically.

steps.title: Run Tests Independently
steps.instruction:
Open the Terminal and run make test yourself. Compare the output to what agent mode reported.

This double-check catches cases where the agent's test run succeeded in a modified environment but fails in a clean one.

steps.title: Check Copilot Logs
steps.instruction:
If agent mode ever gets stuck or produces unexpected output, the Output panel is your diagnostic tool. Select GitHub Copilot from the output dropdown to see the extension's logs.

Look for tool call errors, context length warnings, or model fallback messages.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• Terminal: Ctrl+`
• Output Panel: Ctrl+Shift+U

steps.title: Iterative Refinement
steps.instruction:
Time to refine. Review the code agent mode generated and provide feedback. For example:

The error handling in the inventory route should return a 400 status for invalid query parameters instead of a 500. Also, add input validation for the category parameter.

Agent mode will make the targeted fix and re-run the tests.

steps.title: Try Edit Mode for a Surgical Fix
steps.instruction:
Switch to Edit mode for a small, targeted change. Open your inventory route file in the editor and type:

Add a Swagger/OpenAPI docblock to the GET endpoint so it appears in the API documentation

Edit mode is ideal for single-file refinements after agent mode has done the heavy lifting.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Cross-Stack Rename
steps.instruction:
Let's test agent mode's cross-file coordination. In agent mode, type:

Rename the response field 'stock_level' to 'quantity_available' across the entire inventory feature. Update the API response, TypeScript types, frontend component display, and all test assertions. Run make test after to verify nothing broke.

Watch agent mode coordinate changes across multiple files in a single loop.

steps.title: Review All Changed Files
steps.instruction:
After the rename completes, use Ask mode to verify:

List all files that were changed in this session and summarize the modifications

Verify that every instance was caught. Check the test results to confirm nothing is broken.
steps.tip: Cross-file refactors are one of agent mode's strongest capabilities. A rename that would take a developer 15 minutes of careful find-and-replace takes agent mode one prompt.

steps.title: Inspect the Diff in Source Control
steps.instruction:
Open Source Control and click through the changed files. The diff view shows exactly what was renamed and whether any references were missed.

This is a habit worth building: agent mode proposes, Source Control verifies.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• Source Control: Ctrl+Shift+G

steps.title: Create a Reusable Prompt File
steps.instruction:
Let's save the endpoint scaffolding pattern as a reusable prompt file. Create a new file at .github/prompts/new-api-endpoint.prompt.md:

---
mode: agent
description: Scaffold a new API endpoint with tests
---

Create a new Express route that handles requests to /api/${input:routePath}.

**Requirements:**
- Follow the pattern in api/routes/products.ts
- Register the route in the API entry point
- Create a Vitest test file
- Run make test to verify

Save the file.

steps.title: Run the Prompt File
steps.instruction:
Open Copilot Chat and type / to open the prompt picker. You should see new-api-endpoint in the list.

Select it and enter a route path when prompted (e.g., reports/suppliers).

The prompt file runs with your input and agent mode scaffolds the endpoint. One developer's insight, shared with the whole team.
steps.tip: Prompt files turn your best agent mode workflows into repeatable, team-shared actions. Build a library over time.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Mission Recap
steps.instruction:
Take a moment to review everything you built in this session:

• Explored a full-stack codebase using agent mode reconnaissance
• Set up instruction files tailored to the project's conventions
• Built a backend API endpoint with database queries and tests
• Built a frontend React component with routing and navigation
• Refined code iteratively across all three chat modes
• Performed a cross-stack rename in a single prompt
• Saved reusable prompt files for the team

steps.title: Lab Complete!
steps.instruction:
Mission accomplished! You've completed Agent Potato: License to Chat.

You now know how to:
• Use agent mode to navigate and build across a full-stack codebase
• Write effective prompts with references to existing code
• Set up instructions that shape every Copilot response
• Switch between Ask, Edit, and Agent modes for the right task
• Create reusable prompt files for your team

Next courses: MCP Maximizer to extend agent mode's tools, or Coding Agent: It's Alive! for cloud-based autonomous agents.
